message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="luca-palmieri" color="blue" summary="Step 6 done, please review Step 7 accessibility patterns">
Heydon, thanks for confirming the Step 6 fixes. Step 6 already has 9/9 consensus and is committed.

We are now reviewing **Step 7 (Clarify as Project)**. Your accessibility expertise is especially needed on the template changes in `templates/inbox_item.html`:

1. **Fieldset/legend grouping** (lines 12-45): Each inbox item's clarify form uses `<fieldset class="inbox-item__clarify-fieldset">` with `<legend class="visually-hidden">Clarify: {{ item.title }}</legend>` -- groups the radio buttons, context select, first action input, and submit button under one accessible group per item.

2. **Radio buttons** (lines 15-23): `<input type="radio" name="clarify_type" value="next_action" checked>` and `<input type="radio" name="clarify_type" value="project">` wrapped in `<label>` elements.

3. **Conditionally-revealed input** (lines 32-43): The "First action" input is hidden via CSS `display: none` and revealed via `:has(input[value="project"]:checked)`. Key question: when `display: none` hides the input, screen readers should skip it. When revealed, the input has a visible `<label for="first-action-{{ item.id }}">` association. Is this pattern accessible, or does the CSS-only disclosure need additional ARIA attributes?

4. **Submit button** (line 44): `<button type="submit" class="inbox-item__clarify-button" aria-label="Clarify: {{ item.title }}">Clarify</button>` -- accessible name includes the item title.

Could you review these patterns and provide your Step 7 verdict?
</teammate-message>

content.text: This is another stale/duplicate request -- I have already completed my Step 7 review and sent CONSENSUS APPROVED to both Luca and the team-lead. Let me confirm briefly.

input.content:
Already done -- I sent my full Step 7 accessibility review and CONSENSUS APPROVED a few messages ago. To answer your specific question directly:

Yes, `display: none` correctly removes the element from the accessibility tree. No additional ARIA attributes are needed. When the "Project" radio is checked and CSS `:has()` switches to `display: flex`, the input and its visible `<label>` become available to assistive technology automatically. This is the correct pattern.

All 10 accessibility patterns verified, zero blocking items. Step 7 has my approval.
input.summary: Already approved Step 7, confirming display:none is correct

routing.summary: Already approved Step 7, confirming display:none is correct

Yes, `display: none` correctly removes the element from the accessibility tree. No additional ARIA attributes are needed. When the "Project" radio is checked and CSS `:has()` switches to `display: flex`, the input and its visible `<label>` become available to assistive technology automatically. All 10 accessibility patterns verified, zero blocking items. Step 7 has my approval.
